headline: India's Sacred Forests Hold the Secret to Tree Renewal
meta_description: A new study reveals how spiritual forest protection in India's Western Ghats naturally regenerates endangered tree species better than managed farmland.
summary_short: Sacred groves in India's Western Ghats grow twice as many giant tree species as nearby villages, and naturally regenerate the next forest generation through birds.
body:
In villages scattered across India's Western Ghats—a 1,600-kilometer mountain range running down the country's west coast—the tallest trees grow in places no government protects. They thrive in sacred groves, small forest patches where communities have guarded old-growth trees for generations because they believe the trees belong to their gods.

A new study published in PLOS One reveals why these groves matter far beyond spiritual tradition. Researchers from the Applied Environmental Research Foundation walked through Vanzole village measuring giant trees, guided by local experts Namdev and Anant Shivgan. What they discovered surprised them: the four sacred groves nearby held nearly twice the variety of giant-tree species as the village itself, with 12 species appearing only in the groves.

The difference comes down to disturbance. In villages, livestock trample seedlings and foot trails expose soil. In groves, the undisturbed forest floor allows bird-dropped seeds to sprout. Saplings of fishtail palm grew under 91 percent of grove trees but only 47 percent of village trees. For large-seeded species that only hornbills can carry, the gap widened dramatically: 77 percent in groves versus just 5 percent in villages.

These groves function as ecological nurseries and stepping stones across wider landscapes, allowing tigers and leopards to move freely. Yet they face pressure as concrete temples replace simple shrines and young people leave villages, carrying away generations of forest knowledge.
